did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an ensemble of machine learning models (Random Forest, XGBoost, SVM, KNN, and logistic regression) combined with deep learning feature extractors (Deep Convolutional Neural Networks, Capsule Networks, and Vision Transformers) to classify melanoma skin cancer images. The study achieved a classification accuracy of 91.6% on a melanoma skin cancer dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of an ensemble machine learning model for melanoma skin cancer detection. It details a specific methodology using DCNN, Caps-Net, and ViT feature extractors combined with five ML models, reports experimental results (e.g., 91.6% accuracy) on a specific dataset (Kaggle melanoma dataset), and includes sections on methods, results, and discussion. Although published in the proceedings of the International Conference on Machine Learning and Data Engineering (ICMLDE 2023), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Melanoma Skin Cancer Detection Using Ensemble of Machine Learning Models Considering Deep Feature Embeddings
brief_summary: This study addresses the task of melanoma skin cancer detection by classifying images into malignant and benign categories using an ensemble of machine learning models. The researchers utilized Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNN), Capsule Networks (Caps-Net), and Vision Transformers (ViT) as feature extractors to generate deep feature embeddings from the Kaggle melanoma skin cancer dataset. These embeddings were then fed into an ensemble model comprising five machine learning classifiers: Random Forest, XGBoost, SVM, KNN, and logistic regression, combined via a majority voting mechanism. The experimental evaluation demonstrated that the proposed ensemble approach achieved a classification accuracy of 91.6%, outperforming individual state-of-the-art models. The results highlight the effectiveness of combining diverse deep learning feature extractors with ensemble learning for lightweight and accurate skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies images into two categories: malignant melanoma and benign melanoma, using the Kaggle melanoma skin cancer dataset which contains 50% malignant and 50% benign samples.

all_task_types.target: Malignant vs. Benign melanoma skin cancer
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task distinguishing between malignant melanoma and benign melanoma using an ensemble of machine learning models (Random Forest, XGBoost, SVM, KNN, and logistic regression) trained on deep feature embeddings from DCNN, Caps-Net, and ViT. The model outputs discrete class labels via majority voting, achieving 91.6% accuracy.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from Kaggle containing dermoscopy images of skin lesions used for melanoma detection. The study utilized this dataset to extract deep feature embeddings using DCNN, Caps-Net, and ViT models.

use.training: Used to extract feature embeddings via pre-trained models (VGG-19, Caps-Net, ViT) which were then fed into the ensemble machine learning models.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the classification performance of the ensemble model, achieving 91.6% accuracy.
use.other: The dataset is described as having 50% malignant and 50% benign samples in the lesion types section, but specific split counts are not provided in the experimental setup section.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics without statistical significance testing.

experiment_DCNN_VGG19.notes: The proposed ensemble model achieved the highest accuracy among individual models using DCNN features.

experiment_CapsNet.feature_extractor: Caps-Net (random weights initialized using glorot initializer)
experiment_CapsNet.notes: Caps-Net features failed to capture significant information compared to DCNN. The proposed ensemble model still exhibited the highest accuracy for this feature set.

experiment_ViT.notes: The ensemble model obtained the highest accuracy for the ViT-based feature extractor, outperforming individual models.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of Machine Learning models (Random Forest, XGBoost, SVM, KNN, Logistic Regression) utilizing deep feature embeddings from three distinct Deep Learning architectures: VGG-19 (DCNN), Capsule Network (Caps-Net), and Vision Transformer (ViT).
architecture_used.pretraining: VGG-19 model was pre-trained. Caps-Net weights were initialized using the glorot initializer. ViT details on pretraining are not explicitly reported, though it is described as a transformer-based architecture.

VGG-19.description: Used as the primary DCNN feature extractor. Input images passed through convolutional layers, pooling layers, and activation functions to generate 1-dimensional flattened vectors.

Caps-Net.layers: Two 2-dimensional convolutional layers and one capsule layer
Caps-Net.description: Extracts spatial information (shape, size, orientation). Weights initialized with glorot initializer.

ViT.description: Processes images by dividing them into fixed-size patches and applying self-attention mechanisms. Incorporates convolution operations for initial feature embedding.

software_environment.hardware: Dell Precision 7820, Intel Xeon Gold 5215 2.5GHz processor, 96GB RAM, Ubuntu 18.04 64-bit, Nvidia 16GB Quadro RTX5000

classification_or_output_head.description: The ensemble model combines predictions from the five individual ML models (KNN, LR, RF, XGBoost, SVM) trained on the deep feature embeddings. The final class label is determined by the majority vote of the individual model predictions.

preprocessing_techniques: ViT model uses patch extraction with a patch size of 32 × 32
conclusions: The proposed ensemble model, which combines deep feature embeddings from DCNN, Caps-Net, and ViT with five machine learning classifiers (Random Forest, XGBoost, SVM, KNN, and Logistic Regression) via majority voting, serves as a lightweight system for melanoma skin cancer detection.
The ensemble approach significantly improves classification accuracy compared to individual machine learning models and state-of-the-art (SOTA) models.
The proposed model achieved classification accuracies of 91.4%, 89.6%, and 91.6% when using VGG-19 (DCNN), Caps-Net, and ViT feature extractors, respectively.
The ViT-based feature extractor combined with the ensemble model yielded the highest accuracy (91.6%) among the tested configurations.
The study demonstrates the superiority of combining diverse deep learning feature extractors with ensemble learning for accurate and efficient skin cancer diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the Kaggle melanoma skin cancer dataset, which is a public, non-clinical dataset; results may not generalize to real-world clinical populations due to potential dataset bias and lack of external validation.

The dataset contains a perfectly balanced class distribution (50% malignant, 50% benign), which does not reflect the typical prevalence of melanoma in clinical settings and may inflate performance metrics.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed ensemble model over individual models or state-of-the-art approaches.

The paper claims the model is 'lightweight' because it uses pre-trained deep learning models only as feature extractors for traditional machine learning classifiers; however, the computational cost of running DCNN, Caps-Net, and ViT for feature extraction remains high.

Capsule Networks (Caps-Net) performed poorly (89.6% accuracy) compared to DCNN and ViT, suggesting that the specific implementation or feature extraction capability of Caps-Net was insufficient for this task, limiting the robustness of the ensemble approach.
The input image resolution was reduced to 128x128 pixels, which may discard fine-grained dermoscopic details critical for accurate melanoma diagnosis compared to higher-resolution clinical images.

The paper does not report data leakage risks associated with the Kaggle dataset, which is known to contain duplicate or near-duplicate images across training and test splits, potentially leading to overoptimistic performance estimates.
The study is published in a conference proceedings (Procedia Computer Science) rather than a peer-reviewed medical or high-impact AI journal, which may affect the rigor of the peer review process.
